body:
Bead: agent-1023f072 — Tighten cmd/agent boundary enforcement and CI checks

Status: not completed in this pass. Requires upstream refactor work that the
bead description marks as "out of scope except as needed to make the stricter
boundary tests pass". The "as needed" carve-out turns out to be substantial
once the actual current imports are catalogued, which is why I am stopping
short of a partial commit.

== What is done

- Audit of every cmd/agent/*.go file for `internal/<pkg>` imports.
- Cross-check against CONTRACT-003 §"CLI Projection Boundary"
  (docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md:760-771),
  which forbids cmd/ddx-agent from importing internal/core,
  internal/provider/*, internal/tool, internal/session,
  internal/compaction, internal/harnesses, or internal/routing.

== What the strict boundary check would surface today

Forbidden imports currently present in cmd/agent (non-test, production code):

  cmd/agent/main.go
    - internal/compaction        (lines 231-239, 262: compactionCfg builder)
    - internal/provider/openai   (line 1190: oaiProvider.RankModels)
    - internal/tool              (lines 851-862: BuiltinToolsForPreset,
                                  BashOutputFilterConfig)
  cmd/agent/provider_build.go
    - internal/core              (agent.* construction helpers)
  cmd/agent/routing_provider.go
    - internal/core              (agent.Provider failover wrappers)
  cmd/agent/routing_smart.go
    - internal/core
    - internal/provider/openai
    - internal/session

A test that fails on these imports is one line. The blocker is AC3, which
requires `go test ./cmd/agent -run "...Boundary..."` to pass: writing the
strict assertion without first removing the imports turns the test suite
red, which the bead instructions explicitly forbid ("Do not commit red
code").

== Why the implied refactor is not feasible in one pass here

1. main.go (2079 lines) — moving `compaction.DefaultConfig()` wiring into
   the service requires extending ServiceExecuteRequest semantics or
   ServiceConfig, which the bead notes is itself a contract change
   ("blocker to fix in code or by extending the public contract first").
   Same for `tool.BuiltinToolsForPreset` (CLI today materialises the tool
   list from a preset; the service already accepts ToolPreset, so a
   cleanup is plausible but is a real refactor with a behavioural surface
   to verify, not a typo).
   `oaiProvider.RankModels` is used in catalog ranking; the public
   ListModels surface does not yet expose pattern-ranked candidates, so
   removal requires a public method.

2. routing_smart.go / routing_provider.go / provider_build.go are exactly
   the files ADR-005 §"Migration Plan" item 3 plans to delete (see
   docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-005-smart-routing-replaces-model-routes.md
   lines 78-79, 120). They are tracked as ADR-005 children
   (the parent bead `agent-10f643cc` was filed with that migration in
   mind). Refactoring them here would step on that workstream.

== What a follow-up attempt needs

Order-dependent prerequisites before this bead can land cleanly:

  a. Land the `model_routes` deletion / routing CLI cleanup from ADR-005
     migration item 3 — this removes routing_smart.go, routing_provider.go,
     and provider_build.go, which collectively account for every forbidden
     `internal/core` and `internal/session` import in cmd/agent.

  b. Move the compaction-config build out of main.go. Either:
       - thread context window + reserve + percent through ServiceConfig
         and let the service compute compaction defaults, or
       - expose a `CompactionDefaults()` accessor on the public agent
         package that does not pull internal/compaction symbols across.

  c. Replace `oaiProvider.RankModels` with a public service method
     (e.g. RankModels on DdxAgent or a free function in the public agent
     package). The current call site is in catalog ranking and is small.

  d. Decide whether internal/tool.BuiltinToolsForPreset has a public
     replacement (it largely overlaps with what the service already does
     when given ToolPreset). If yes, drop the CLI-side preset materialisation
     entirely. If no, promote the helpers to the public `agent` package.

Once (a)-(d) are done, the strict test is a small change:

  - Drop core, provider/openai, session, tool, compaction from the
    allowlist in cmd/agent/service_boundary_test.go (leaving config,
    modelcatalog, observations, productinfo, prompt, reasoning, safefs).
  - Add a `TestCLIInternalImportBoundaryDenylist` that explicitly fails
    on any import containing /internal/core, /internal/provider/,
    /internal/session, /internal/tool, /internal/compaction,
    /internal/harnesses, /internal/routing — covering symbols not just
    packages (e.g. agentcore.Run, agentcore.Provider).
  - Add a CI gate entry (Makefile target or GH Actions step) that runs
    these specific tests separately from the full `go test ./...` so a
    boundary regression is unambiguous in the CI summary.

== Recommendation

Reopen this bead as a child of (or successor to) the ADR-005 migration
item 3 ("Config + CLI cleanup + deprecation"). Once the routing-smart /
routing_provider / provider_build files are gone and main.go's tool +
compaction wiring is rerouted, this bead's test changes are ~30 lines and
pass cleanly without any additional refactor noise.

Verification commands attempted in this pass:
  go test ./cmd/agent -run "TestCLI(ServiceContractUsesTypedEventDecoder|MainDoesNotImportOrCallInternalCoreRun|InternalImportBoundaryAllowlist)"
  -> ok (pre-existing baseline, captured at b5920e2)

APPROVE
The boundary spec is properly tightened: production allowlist is reduced to seven internals with no public replacement, an absolute forbidden-import list bans the core/provider/session/tool/compaction/harnesses/routing packages across both production and test files, and a forbidden-symbol scan catches alias-based re-leaks. CLI code paths now go through a new `public_cli_api.go` re-export, and the meta-test guards against accid…

The boundary spec is properly tightened: production allowlist is reduced to seven internals with no public replacement, an absolute forbidden-import list bans the core/provider/session/tool/compaction/harnesses/routing packages across both production and test files, and a forbidden-symbol scan catches alias-based re-leaks. CLI code paths now go through a new `public_cli_api.go` re-export, and the meta-test guards against accidental allowlist removal.
